points: Matter is composed of extremely small constituent particles that cannot be seen even with an ordinary microscope. These particles are the basic building blocks of all substances, as demonstrated when chalk is broken down into progressively smaller pieces and sugar dissolves invisibly in water. Interparticle spaces—empty regions between particles—allow dissolved substances to occupy available space within other materials.

Interparticle attractions are the forces that hold constituent particles together. The strength of these forces depends on the nature of the substance and the distance between particles; even slight increases in distance drastically decrease the attractive forces. These attractions fundamentally determine whether a substance exists as a solid, liquid, or gas.

summary.category: Properties of Different States of Matter
points: Solids have strong interparticle attractions that hold particles in fixed positions, resulting in definite shape and volume. Particles vibrate around fixed positions but cannot move past each other, making solids rigid and unable to be compressed or easily deformed without breaking.

Liquids have weaker interparticle attractions than solids, allowing particles to move freely past each other while remaining closely packed. This gives liquids a definite volume but no fixed shape—they take the shape of their container. Liquids can flow and diffuse but resist compression due to particles being close together.

Gases have very weak interparticle attractions with particles far apart and moving rapidly in all directions. Gases have neither definite shape nor definite volume; they spread to fill all available space. Gases are highly compressible because there is significant space between particles, and diffusion occurs rapidly.

Solids are incompressible due to minimal interparticle spacing and strong attractive forces keeping particles tightly packed. Liquids also resist compression because particles are closely spaced, though less tightly than in solids. Gases are highly compressible because particles have large interparticle spaces and weak attractive forces, allowing particles to be pushed much closer together.

The interparticle spacing determines both the physical properties and state of matter. Solids have the smallest spacing (particles tightly packed), liquids have intermediate spacing (particles close but mobile), and gases have the largest spacing (particles far apart and free-moving). Temperature affects particle motion and can cause phase transitions by overcoming or reducing interparticle attractions.

Particles in all states of matter are in constant motion, with the kinetic energy of particles varying by state: solid particles vibrate in place, liquid particles move freely within the liquid, and gas particles move rapidly in all directions. This motion, driven by thermal energy, is fundamental to understanding why matter behaves as it does in different states.

Diffusion is the spreading of particles from high concentration areas to low concentration areas, occurring most rapidly in gases (which spread quickly and evenly throughout space) and slowly in solids (where particles are fixed in position). In liquids, diffusion occurs at intermediate rates. This universal process demonstrates that matter particles are constantly moving and demonstrates the particulate nature of all substances.
